coredata.dc:title: A design strategy of dual-ratiomentric optical probe based on europium-doped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent visual detection of anthrax biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Qiuju
Fang, Yajun
Li, Jiayu
Hong, Dan
Zhu, Panpan
Chen, Shihong
Tan, Kejun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel dual-ratiometric optical probe based on europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) was developed for colorimetric and fluorescent visual sensing dipicolinic acid (DPA), a anthrax biomarker. Eu-CDs with recessive signals were prepared via thermal pyrolysis method. And the magenta appears when Eu-CDs coordinates with Eriochrome Black T (EBT). Based on the ligand displacement strategy, DPA reduces magenta and makes blue appear, which develop ratiometric colorimetric visual detection methods of DPA. Under 270 nm excitation, DPA make the red fluorescence signal of Eu in EBT-CDs@Eu appear, based on the absorbance-energy transfer-emission effect, the ratiometric fluorescent visual detection assay of DPA has been developed. Based above, dual-ratiometric optical probe was designed successfully. The limit of detection (LOD) is 10.6 nM for ratio colorimetric assay. More prominently, naked-eye determination of DPA without instrument is as low as 1.0 μM. Furthermore, its practicability was verified by quantifying the DPA in Bacillus subtilis spores and human urine (with RSD≤2.35%). The sensor shows great superiority in on-site analysis of DPA, especially in limited instruments conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: EBT-CDs@Eu
Dual-ratiometric
Dipicolinic acid
Visual sensing
Eu-CDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the EBT-CDs@Eu probe and determination of the DPA
Detection of DPA in bacterial spore samples
Cytotoxicity test and cell imaging for Eu-CDs
Smartphone application for the detection of DPA
Selectivity and interference of DPA detection
Detection of DPA in bacterial spores
Detection of DPA in urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the Eu-CDs. (b) XRD pattern (c) FT-IR spectrum and (d) XPS spectrum of Eu-CDs.
Cell image of Eu-CDs. (a–c) Eu-CDs with A549 cells. (d–f) Controlled experiment.
Effect of (a) pH (b) temperature (c) reaction time and (d) ionic strength on the absorption intensity of A625/A535. c DPA, 10 μM.

(a) UV–vis spectra of EBT, EBT-CDs@Eu, EBT-CDs@Eu + DPA (2 μM and 10 μM) and DPA (b) FL spectra of EBT, EBT-CDs@Eu and EBT-CDs@Eu + DPA. (c) Lifetime of fluorescence emission at 445 nm and (d) 615 nm of the EBT-CDs@Eu with and without DPA.

(a) Absorbance response of EBT-CDs@Eu with DPA (0–12 μM). (b) Curves of absorption ratio (A625/A535) with the increase of DPA concentration. (c) Visual color changes of EBT-CDs@Eu with different concentrations of DPA (in the daylight). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence spectra (λex = 270 nm) of the EBT-CDs@Eu with DPA. (b) Fitting curve of fluorescence intensity ratio (F615/F445) with the increase of DPA concentration. (c) Visual color changes of EBT-CDs@Eu with DPA (Under the excitation of 254 nm UV lamp). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram of Red channel/Blue channel obtained by using portable smartphone. (b) Red channel/Blue channel absorption ratio (A625/A535) change versus concentration of DPA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Selectivity analysis of EBT-CDs@Eu for DPA detection. (a) Absorbance ratio (A625/A535) and (b) fluorescence ratio (F615/F445).c DPA: 10 μM. (c) Photos of EBT-CDs@Eu with different interferences under daylight (upper) and the excitation of 254 nm UV lamp (bottom).

(a) Absorbance response of EBT-CDs@Eu upon different volumes of Bacillus subtilis spores (0–250 μL). (b) Calibration curve between absorbance values (A625/A535) and various volume of DPA Bacillus subtilis spores.
Schematic illustration for the reaction mechanism between EBT-CDs@Eu and DPA.
The comparison of different methods for DPA detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Naked-eye recognition means the lowest concentration of DPA that can be recognized by naked-eye without any instrument.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of DPA in real samples.

head.ce:title: A design strategy of dual-ratiomentric optical probe based on europium-doped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent visual detection of anthrax biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Translational Medicine in Major Metabolic Disease, The First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 400016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Translational Medicine in Major Metabolic Disease
The First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Translational Medicine in Major Metabolic Disease, The First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel dual-ratiometric optical probe based on europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) was developed for colorimetric and fluorescent visual sensing dipicolinic acid (DPA), a anthrax biomarker. Eu-CDs with recessive signals were prepared via thermal pyrolysis method. And the magenta appears when Eu-CDs coordinates with Eriochrome Black T (EBT). Based on the ligand displacement strategy, DPA reduces magenta and makes blue appear, which develop ratiometric colorimetric visual detection methods of DPA. Under 270 nm excitation, DPA make the red fluorescence signal of Eu in EBT-CDs@Eu appear, based on the absorbance-energy transfer-emission effect, the ratiometric fluorescent visual detection assay of DPA has been developed. Based above, dual-ratiometric optical probe was designed successfully. The limit of detection (LOD) is 10.6 nM for ratio colorimetric assay. More prominently, naked-eye determination of DPA without instrument is as low as 1.0 μM. Furthermore, its practicability was verified by quantifying the DPA in Bacillus subtilis spores and human urine (with RSD≤2.35%). The sensor shows great superiority in on-site analysis of DPA, especially in limited instruments conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by the Bacillus anthracis, which can result in death upon inhalation of more than 104 spores without effective medical treatment within 24–28 h [1,2]. What's more, Bacillus anthracis spores were used as delivery vehicles in anthrax attacks in 2001 [3]. However, anthrax is difficult to diagnose immediately because it takes 1–60 days for anthrax symptoms to appear in humans. Dipicolinic acid (DPA) is the main component in protective layers of bacterial spore (5–15% of the dry mass of the spores). Therefore, DPA becomes a unique biomarker of Bacillus anthracis spores or anthracis disease [4]. Due to its high toxicity and lethality, it is still of great significance to explore a efficient, selective and sensitive method for DPA detection.

Up to now, various methods have been reported for DPA sensing, such as polymerase chain reaction [5], immunoassays [6], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [7], molecular imprinting [8], and so on. However, due to the expensive consumables, complex operation process and multi-step sample preparation, these shortcomings may become obstacles in practical application. Recently, lanthanide-based probes, especially those involving Eu3+ or Tb3+, on account of theirs unique optical and spectral properties such as long fluorescence lifetimes, narrow line-like emission bands and large stokes shift, have been widely applied for DPA detection [9–11]. However, trivalent lanthanide ions (Ln3+) have low absorption coefficients due to the Laporte forbidden f-f transition, but the designed Ln3+ probes can improve the Laporte forbidden f-f transition through absorbance-energy transfer-emission (AETE) effect, or aromatic ligands (antenna effect) [12,13]. The AETE effect is originated from lanthanide-ligand complex. Ln3+ fluorescence can be markedly enhanced by energy transfer of chelating ligands. Kim prepared the water-dispersible terbium-metal organic framework (Tb-MOF) as a fluorescent probe for DPA detection [9]. Donmez designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe of EDTA-Eu3+complex for selectivity and sensitivity detection of DPA [14].Chen reported a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on europium (Eu)-doped silicon nanoparticles(Eu@SiNPs) for high selectivity and sensitivity detection of DPA [15]. However, most of the methods depend on a single signal response, resulting in poor visual detection effect. Although some methods can be used for ratiometric colorimetric detection, the visual effect is still unsatisfactory [16,17]. So, it is still necessary to further improve the accuracity and sensitivity of visual detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) are attractive emerging materials in recent years. They have many excellent properties, such as high photostability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy synthesis [18–20] etc., and are therefore widely used in fluorescent probe [21], bio-imaging [22] and drug delivery [23], etc. Based on their excellent properties, CDs were also used to design sensor for the detection of DPA [24–26]. But in these applications, most of the sensors rely on a single photoluminescence signal that varies in intensity, are not sensitive enough to their color variations with the target, and most of the color changes with targets can be observed with the aid of instruments, which is not conducive to their extensive applications in the real sample detection [16,17,27]. On the contrary, the ratiometric probe can solve the above problems well, especially the dual-ratiometric probe of colorimetric and fluorescence, which can achieve accurate response to the target and has good visualization effect. Surprisingly, at present, most of the DPA sensors are single ratiometric probes, and there is hardly research on dual-ratiometric determination of DPA [25–27]. Therefore, for realize accurate and on-site DPA sensing, dual-ratiometric probe of colorimeric and fluorescent is worth exploring.

Herein, in this work, based on the ligand displacement strategy and AETE effect, we designed an dual-ratiometric optical probe (EBT-CDs@Eu) for high sensitivity colorimetric and fluorescent sensing of DPA (Scheme 1 ). EBT is a commercial metal complexing agent for complexometric titration of alkaline earth and lanthanides. The Eu-CDs were synthesized from glutathione, formamid and europium nitrate by one-pot pyrolysis method. The prepared Eu-CDs have blue fluorescence with excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. In the presence of DPA, the EBT-CDs@Eu produce a color change from magenta to blue and a red characteristic fluorescence emission was obtained from DPA-CDs@Eu. So, a simple and cost-effective method for the dual-ratiometric visual detection of DPA has been developed. And the present sensor has been successfully applied for the determination of DPA in human urine and spore lysate samples.

Eriochrome Black T was purchased from Chengdu Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Sichuan, China). Europium nitrate hexahydrate, glutathione and formamid were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA) was purchased from Beijing Huaweiruike Chemical Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Bacillus subtilis (CMCC (B) 63501) was purchased from Shanghai Luwei Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM), Hepes buffer (10 mM), phosphate buffer saline (PBS) buffer and Mops buffer (10 mM) were used to control the acidity of system. Ultra-pure water obtained from a Milli-Q ultrapure (18.2 MΩ cm) system. Unless otherwise stated, reagents and solvents were reagent grade and used directly without purification. In addition, the apparatus used in the experiment were detailed in Supporting Information.

The Eu-CDs can be rapidly prepared by one-pot solvothermal method. 30 mg of europium nitrate hexahydrate, 330 mg of glutathione and 4.16 mL of formamide were transferred into a stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 40 min. The product was naturally cooled to room temperature. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 10000r for 10 min to remove insoluble particles. The obtained solution was purified by 72 h dialyzing with a 500 Da membrane and then stored at 4 °C for further use.

The operation process of cytotoxicity test was detailed in Supporting Information. And the detailed operation of cell imaging experiment is as follows: A549 cells were employed for Eu-CDs fluorescence imaging. And the detailed operation is as follows: First, the A549 cells (3 × 106 cells/mL) were seeded and incubated in a constant temperature incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C) for 24 h. Next, the A549 cells were cultured with the 2.0 mg/mL Eu-CDs solutions for 48 h and then washed three times with PBS buffer to remove the free Eu-CDs. The cell imaging was observed on an inverted fluorescence microscope under 200X lens. The same procedure was used in the control experiment without adding Eu-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the EBT-CDs@Eu probe and determination of the DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of EBT (10 mM) and DPA (10 mM) were prepared. Firstly, 150 μL EBT solution (from 10 mM stock solution), 165 μL Eu-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and 100 μL Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) were added in centrifugation tube, and the dual-ratiometric EBT-CDs@Eu probe has been simply prepared. Then, different concentrations of DPA (from 10 mM stock solution) were added into the above prepared solutions, and diluted with ultra-pure water to 1.0 mL, followed by vortex shaking. Finally, the mixtures were reacted for 10 min at 20 °C. Controlled experiments were carried out using ultra-pure water instead of DPA. And the absorbance and fluorescence intensities of each sample were measured.

To evaluate the practicality of EBT-CDs@Eu probe, human urine was used as real sample for determination of DPA. The urine was collected from the healthy people and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant solution was employed as a stock solution. The urine samples were diluted 50-fold before analysis without further pretreatment. Then, the samples were added into a series of known DPA concentrations by standard addition method for further determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in bacterial spore samples
ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus subtilis spores were employed as the source of DPA. The procedure was performed based on the literature [28]. The second generation Bacillus subtilis was seeded in agar containing 20 g/L of glucose, 15 g/L of yeast peptone, 0.5 g/L of beef extract and 5 g/L of NaCl at 37 °C for 24 h. After spore formation, the spores were scraped off the agar plate and suspended in ultra-pure water. The spores were washed 5 times with ultra-pure water and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min to ensure the complete removal of the culture medium. Then, the spore suspensions were transferred into centrifugal tubes and dodecylamine was added. Afterward, the suspensions were heated to 90 °C for 30 min to germinate the spores and release DPA completely. The spore solutions were diluted 3-fold for absorbance determination and 0-fold for fluorescence determination, and the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of mixtures were recorded.

The morphology and microstructure of Eu-CDs were characterized by TEM image. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the Eu-CDs were well dispersed and regular in shape. The diameter of the Eu-CDs was about 3.0–7.7 nm (Fig. S1). By randomly counting 100 particles, the average diameter of Eu-CDs is 5.1 nm. The element composition ratios of C, N, O, S and Eu in Eu-CDs are 0.5328, 0.1762, 0.2513, 0.0317 and 0.008, respectively (Table S1). The XRD pattern (Fig. 1b) of the Eu-CDs shows a broad peak around 24°, which is attributed to highly disordered of carbon atoms. The broad diffraction peak may be due to the weak interactions between the layers in the graphite-like nanostructures [29]. Furthermore, the Eu-CDs is negatively charged, and zeta potential is −1.78 mV in water and −4.33 mV in Tris-HCl 7.0 buffer (Fig. S2, Fig. S3). Vast amino and carboxylic groups on the Eu-CDs’ surface may be the reason for the lower potential of Eu-CDs.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was used to study the surface functional groups present in the Eu-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1c, the broad peak at 3650-3120 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. These functional hydrophilic groups on the surface greatly improve the water solubility of Eu-CDs. The stretching vibrations of CN and CC are located at 1633 cm−1, and the peak emerged at 1047 cm−1 can be assigned to the tensile vibration of oxidized S bonds and CS. The typical peaks at 1689 and 1396 cm−1 are derived from the amidic CO and C–N vibrations respectively. The observed peak at 1315 cm−1 belongs to the C–N valence bond vibrations.

Furthermore, the chemical states of the Eu-CDs were investigated by XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). The detailed analysis process is shown in Supporting Information and Fig. S4, the results of XPS is consistent with the FTIR. Meanwhile, the optical properties of Eu-CDs were also studied, which was detailed in Supporting Information (Figs. S5–S10). All the experimental results reveal that the Eu-CDs have exceptional optical properties, which makes it possible to be applied in complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity test and cell imaging for Eu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the good biocompatibility of the Eu-CDs, cytotoxicity and cell imaging of Eu-CDs were studied in organism. The cytotoxicity was evaluated by Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay with A549 cells. As shown in Fig. S11, when the concentration of Eu-CDs is as high as 0.8 mg/mL, the cell viability is still greater than 96%, which indicates that the Eu-CDs have low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Then, the cell imaging experiment was carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 , A549 cells still maintain good morphology and bright fluorescence when the concentration of Eu-CDs is up to 2.0 mg/mL. These results confirm that the Eu-CDs can be employed for cellular imaging in biological system.

The Eu-CDs not only has excellent optical properties, but also has specific response to DPA, the prepared Eu-CDs were employed to prepare dual-ratiometric probe with EBT (EBT-CDs@Eu) for the determination of DPA. The mechanism of the proposed method for ratiometric colorimetric and fluorescence detection of DPA is presented in Scheme 1.

In order to obtain the best analytical parameters, the effects of acidity, temperature, reaction time and ionic strength on the detection of DPA were investigated, as shown in Fig. 3 , and the detailed optimization process is displayed in section optimization of detection conditions in Supporting Information. After optimization, the optimal determination conditions are Tris-HCl buffer of pH 7.0, temperature of 20 °C, reaction time of 10 min, and no addition of NaCl.

To discuss the sensing mechanism of EBT-CDs@Eu on DPA, the UV–vis absorption spectra of EBT, Eu-CDs, DPA, EBT-CDs@Eu and EBT-CDs@Eu with DPA were studied (Fig. 4 a). EBT solution is blue in pH 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer (Fig. 4a, inset). After adding Eu-CDs, the solution color clearly changed immediately from blue to magenta (Fig. 4a, inset), and a new absorption peak appeared at 535 nm. The subsequent addition of DPA instantly makes the solution color change from magenta to purple to blue (Fig. 4a, inset), and the absorption peak position is accompanied by a red shift of about 90 nm (from 535 to 625 nm). It is due to the stronger binding ability of DPA to Eu (III) than EBT. When EBT solution is added to the Eu-CDs, the EBT can chelate Eu (III) and form EBT-CDs@Eu resulting in color change from blue to magenta accompanying with the rise of 535 nm absorption peak and the decrease of 625 nm absorption peak. The 535 nm absorption peak can be attributed to EBT-CDs@Eu and the 625 nm absorption peak is attributed to EBT [26]. After the addition of DPA, it can displace EBT from EBT-CDs@Eu and form DPA-CDs@Eu, and EBT is released into solution, resulting in color changes from magenta to purple to blue accompanying with the recovery of 625 nm absorption peak and the decrease of 535 nm absorption peak. The results reveal the sensing mechanism is the ligand displacement mechanism of DPA to EBT [26,30] (See Scheme 1). The results were also evident from FT-IR studies (Fig. S12). The notable color changes can be used for the naked-eye detection of DPA. And the obvious changes of absorption peaks can be used for the ratiometric colorimetric sensing of DPA.

To further explore its fluorescence sensing mechanism, we have studied the fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs, EBT, EBT-CDs@Eu and EBT-CDs@Eu + DPA (Fig. 4b). Under 270 nm excitation (DPA has absorption peak at 270 nm, Fig. 4a red line), the EBT-CDs@Eu exhibits blue fluorescence at 445 nm, the blue fluorescence of the EBT-CDs@Eu can be quenched by DPA, meanwhile, the red fluorescence of the Eu-CDs can be observed (Fig. 4b). As the concentration of DPA increases, the blue fluorescence intensity decreases and the red fluorescence intensity increases correspondingly (Fig. 6a). And the photoluminescence color changes from blue to red, which can be easily recognized under an UV lamp (Fig. 4b, inset). Thus, an effective ratiometric fluorescence sensor of DPA was developed. DPA is a tridentate ligand and has an excellent “antenna effect”, which can promote the f-f transition of Eu (III) and results in great fluorescent enhancement of Eu (III) [31]. Generally, Eu can coordinate ligand resulting in red fluorescence signals at about 593 and 615 nm via antenna effect [32]. So the Eu in Eu-CDs is a recessive signal. When DPA is added, the DPA-CDs@Eu is formed due to the coordination of DPA with Eu (III), and the solution emits intense red fluorescence at 593 and 615 nm, which can be assigned to the 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 0,1,2) transition [33,34]. And the maximum fluorescence signal is observed at 615 nm, which can be attributed to the 5D0 → 7F2 transition [33].

The blue fluorescence of the EBT-CDs@Eu can be quenched by DPA. Quenching mechanism can be divided into static quenching, dynamic quenching, förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), surface energy transfer (SET), dexter energy transfer (DET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [35,36]. For static quenching, the formation of the ground-state complex can result in the change of the absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2b, the UV absorption curve of EBT-CDs@Eu changes significantly after adding DPA, and the fluorescence lifetime of 445 nm decreases slightly (from 3.845 to 3.563 ns) after the addition of 70 μM DPA (Fig. 2c) indicating that DPA and EBT-CDs@Eu form weak or non-luminous ground state complex. Thus, quenching mechanism of Eu-CDs after adding DPA should be a static quenching process [37]. On the other hand, DPA molecule (Fig. S13) consists of two functional groups, a pyridine ring and two carboxyl groups, respectively. Owing to its electron withdrawing nature, this pyridine ring may capture excited electrons from surrounding environment, such as EBT-CDs@Eu. Such electron-transfer process may quench the excited state energy of these electrons, leading to fluorescence quenching of EBT-CDs@Eu [38]. The fluorescence lifetime of 445 nm decreases from 3.845 to 3.563 ns when DPA concentration increases from 0 to 70 μM, indicating an electron-transfer process, from Eu-CDs to DPA (pyridine ring) [39]. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of EBT-CDs@Eu increased from - 15.7 mV to - 5.9 mV in the absence and presence of DPA (Fig. S14). The results show that the carboxyl group of Eu-CDs in EBT-CDs@Eu directly acts on the positively charged nitrogen atoms of the DPA molecule through an electrostatic interaction [40], This will causes changes in charge distribution and energy, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of EBT-CDs@Eu. The experimental results further verify the electron-transfer process. So, the quenching mechanism of EBT-CDs@Eu by DPA is the combination effect of electron transfer process and the formation of weak or non-luminescent ground state complexes.

To better understand the interaction between EBT-CDs@Eu and DPA, the fluorescence lifetime at 616 nm of EBT-CDs@Eu were measured in the presence and absence of DPA. As shown in 4d, the fluorescence lifetime of EBT-CDs@Eu at 615 nm decreases from 1.147 to 1.045 ns. This can be attributed to the situation that the O–H vibration of water molecules leads to nonradiation deactivation and makes the fluorescence lifetime of Eu (III) decreased [41]. Under the excitation of 270 nm, DPA can absorb energy and transfer it to Eu (III) [42]. The slight decrease of fluorescence lifetime also indicates that the efficiency of absorbance-energy transfer from DPA to Eu (III) is not very high. The results certified the detection mechanism is AETE effect from DPA to Eu (III) [41,43,44].

Under the optimum conditions, the absorbance spectra of different concentrations DPA with EBT-CDs@Eu were recorded, as shown in Fig. 5 a. It is found that with the raise of DPA content, the absorption intensity of EBT-CDs@Eu increases at 625 nm and decreases at 535 nm. Fig. 5b exhibits the corresponding relationship between A625/A535 and DPA concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 12.0 μM. The regression equation is A625/A535 = 2.297-1.818exp (−0.07233c DPA) (μM) and the correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9984. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 10.64 nM (3σ), which was far lower than the infectious dosage (60 μM) of the Bacillus anthracis spores [45]. Furthermore, the visual color change can be easily distinguished by naked-eye without any instrument after adding 1.0 μM DPA (Fig. 5c), which is an order of magnitude lower than previously reported [26,27].

Similar to absorption spectra, the fluorescence intensity of EBT-CDs@Eu increases at 616 nm and decreases at 445 nm with the increase of DPA content from 0.5 to 110.0 μM, as shown in Fig. 6a. When DPA is 12.0 μM, the fluorescent color changes can be distinguished obviously by naked eyes with a UV lamp. Fig. 6b shows the calibration curves of fluorescence ratio (F616/F445) versus DPA concentrations. The linear equation is F616/F445 = 4.741-4.734exp (−0.02331c DPA) (μM) and the correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9992. The LOD was calculated to be 50 nM. The fluorescence color changes obviously from blue to purple and then to red with the increase of DPA concentration under the excitation of 254 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6c), which is consistent with the CIE coordinate (Fig. S15).

To demonstrate the advantages of proposed method, we compared it with other reported methods, as shown in Table 1 , most of reported methods about DPA detection at present are single-ratiometric detection, and a few assays with naked eye recognition has no satisfactory sensitivity. It is verified that the designed dual-ratiometric optical probe has the advantages of novelty, high sensitivity and accuracy. And the LOD of this method is quite competitive. More importantly, the proposed assay can be used to detect lower concentration DPA (1.0 μM) by naked-eye without using any instrument, which is the lowest detection concentration of DPA for naked-eye known to us by now. Thus, it will be a promising tool for the naked-eye and on-site detection of DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for the detection of DPA
ce:section.ce:para: When the concentration of DPA increases from 0 to 12 μM, the EBT-CDs@Eu presents continuous color changes from magenta to blue under daylight. So we can use a smartphone to detect DPA (Fig. 7 a). Different concentrations (0–12 μM) DPA was added into the EBT-CDs@Eu solution, and then photos were taken by a smartphone under daylight. Using a smartphone (iphone) with color scanning application (Photoshop, Adobe), and the B channel and R channel data (RGB color model) of photos were quickly obtained. The value of channel B/R were calculated to evaluate the change of photos’ absorbance intensities with different DPA contents. As shown in Fig. 7b, the R channel/B channel has a good proportion correlation (R2 = 0.9984) with the concentrations of DPA, and the LOD is as low as 0.68 μM, which makes it possible to carry out semi-quantitative detection of DPA using only a portable smart-phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of DPA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed sensing system, the effects of several compounds on the EBT-CDs@Eu responses of absorbance and fluorescence under the optimized conditions were investigated. Including benzoic acid (BA), p-phthalic acid (p-PA), isophthalic acid (I-PA), 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (Btc), 2-picolinic acid, pyridine-2,3-dicarboxylic acid (Quinolinic acid), nicotinic acid (NA), isonicotinic acid, pyridine-3,5-dicarboxylic acid (Nicotine acid), pyridine-2,4-dicarboxylic acid (Lutidinic acid), cysteine, histidine, lysine, tryptophan, glycine, KCl and Na2CO3, and the absorbance and fluorescence intensities were measured. The final concentrations of all the tested substances were 500 μM. The absorbance and fluorescence ratios of various compounds are shown in Fig. 8 . The results show that only the addition of DPA leads to significant ratiometric responses. Importantly, its color changes are too obvious and can be recognized by the naked eye (Fig. 8, inset). The results demonstrate that EBT-CDs@Eu probe has excellent selectivity for the determination of DPA. Meanwhile, the interference of co-existing substances was also investigated. Detailed experimental process and results are shown in section effects of co-existence substances and Fig. S16 in Supporting Information. The results show that the developed assay of DPA exhibits a good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in bacterial spores
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate its potential application, the proposed assay of ratio colorimetric was applied for quantitative evaluation of DPA in bacterial spores. As shown in Fig. 9a and S17a, with the increase of spore solutions volume (corresponding to DPA concentration), the intensities of absorbance and fluorescence changed immediately. According to the literature, each spore of Bacillus subtilis contains 3.65 × 10−16 mol of DPA [49]. So, we can indirectly quantify the number of spores on the basis of the amount of DPA in the unknown suspension. The absorbance intensity ratio of A625/A535 increases correspondingly with the increase of spore solutions volume from 0 to 250 μL (Fig. 9 a). The LOD is 1.18 μL (corresponding to 3.4 × 107 Bacillus subtilis spores). The fluorescence intensity ratio of A625/A535 increases linearly with the increase of spore solutions volume from 0 to 675 μL (Fig. S17b). The LOD is 0.096 μL (corresponding to 2.5 × 107 Bacillus subtilis spores). The above results indicate that the developed method can be used in real samples analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the practicability of the proposed method, urine was also chosen as a real sample for DPA detection. The results obtained by the standard addition method were shown in Table 2 , it can be seen that the recoveries ranged from 98.89% to 106.02%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 2.35%. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method has good accuracy and precision, which ensures its practicability and reliability for detecting DPA in real samples.

In summary, Eu-CDs with desirable properties has been successfully synthesized by a simple one-pot solvothermal method and applied to DPA visual detection and cell imaging. The prepared Eu-CDs have excellent water solubility, stability and biocompatibility. On these bases, an dual-ratiometric EBT-CDs@Eu probe was constructed for the colorimetric and fluorescent muti-color sensing of DPA. After further confirmation, the mechanisms of response toward DPA are ligand displacement and AETE effect. The devised probe has distinct merits of straightforward preparation, good specificity and high sensitivity to DPA with a LOD of 10.64 nM, which is over three orders of magnitude lower than the infection dosage of Bacillus anthracis spores. Notably, the proposed assay has superiorities of abundant color changes and naked-eye identification as low as 1.0 μM. Furthermore, this probe has been successfully applied for DPA detection in human urine and Bacillus subtilis spores, which indicates that it has great potential in biological and chemical analysis.

Zhou Qiuju: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing -Original Draft. Fang Yajun: Investigation, Visualization, Validation. Li Jiayu: Resources, Formal analysis. Hong Dan: Formal analysis. Zhu Panpan: Formal analysis. Chen Shihong: Writing - review & editing. Tan Kejun: Resources, Writing -Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
Qiuju Zhou and Yajun Fang have equally contributed to this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, grant No. 21377103) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2019TY003).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121548.
